other_side: This edition's lead rests on Exponential View's audit: $110bn banked over twelve months, a $175bn annualised run rate, quarterly revenue now exceeding CapEx depreciation — offered as proof that AI demand is real and realised, not merely promised. Zitron's piece attacks precisely that framing. The revenue, he argues, is largely circular: hyperscalers pour capital into their own unprofitable labs, OpenAI and Anthropic, which spend it straight back on compute, and only a fraction returns as "revenue" that is really venture subsidy recycled. He notes the hyperscalers won't disclose real AI revenues, hiding behind "vague run rates," and that their capex will outpace cashflow from Q3 2026. Citing the Bank for International Settlements, he warns the build-out serves demand that "does not exist outside of Anthropic and OpenAI" and rests on the hope that today's models will "turn into something completely different."
truth_beneath: Both men orbit one variable: whether the vast AI spend is converting into real, self-sustaining demand and returns, or only the promise of it. Both accept the capex is enormous — roughly $848bn this year, $2tn cumulative — that headline revenue is climbing fast, and that the money flows mainly through a handful of labs. The empirical question is whether that revenue is durable, unsubsidised paying demand; the observables are hyperscaler capex against free cash flow, lab losses against revenue, and whether "run rate" figures survive as audited profit. The residue is worldview: Exponential View reads rising realised revenue and GPUs earning past year six as validation, Zitron reads the same numbers as recycled subsidy propping a bubble. Whittemore is vindicated if the run rate becomes self-funding profit; Zitron if capex outruns cashflow and surfaces as writedowns. Both are polemicists with incentives — NLW bullish and anti-doom, Zitron bubble-certain with a paid newsletter — and the signal is where they agree despite opposite priors. Watch whether Q3 2026 results show hyperscaler capex crossing above free cash flow.
